one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning LLMs (with or without explicit CoT reasoning) for vulnerability detection substantially mitigates but does not eliminate reliance on surface-level functional patterns instead of genuine vulnerability root-cause understanding, and CoT reasoning trades a large drop in recall for improved robustness to paired (vulnerable, patched) code and semantic-preserving perturbations.
key_finding: Three empirically distinct findings: (1) Vanilla SFT (no explicit reasoning) yields deceptively high V2N-to-V2N F1 (e.g., Qwen3-8B 83.65%, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 83.88%) that collapses on V2P-to-V2P (Qwen3-8B 66.06%, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 66.39%); in the V2N-to-V2P cross-dataset setting, recall stays very high (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 96.98%) but FPR explodes (94.38%), showing the model blindly flags patched code because it looks like the vulnerable pattern it memorized. (2) CoT/explicit-reasoning SFT substantially reduces this false-positive collapse (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct FPR drops from 94.38% to 64.60% in V2N-to-V2P) and keeps augmentation-induced F1 degradation mostly under 1.12pp (versus 3.86-7.21pp for vanilla SFT), but at a large recall cost (e.g., deepseek-llm-7b-chat V2P-to-V2P recall drops from 73.45% to 46.31%, F1 from 65.49% to 52.67%). (3) Under vanilla SFT, detection performance on (vulnerable, patched) pairs is weakly but statistically significantly correlated with CodeBLEU semantic-gap score across all five models (Spearman |sc| between 0.033 and 0.074, all p<0.05, e.g. p=3.4084e-6 for Qwen3-8B, p=3.3016e-5 for deepseek-coder-6.7b-instruct); under CoT SFT this correlation becomes statistically non-significant (p>0.05 in all cases, e.g. p=0.349 for Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, p=0.871 for deepseek-llm-7b-chat), but the authors explicitly caution this could be a floor effect from compressed recall rather than genuine escape from the trap. The reasoning-failure taxonomy shows CoT models still commonly misinterpret control flow (C1, driving false positives) and misapply vulnerability/CWE definitions (C5, driving false negatives), with broadly similar failure-category distributions across all five models and settings.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TrapEval, an evaluation framework that dissects the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) process itself -- rather than out-of-the-box/zero-shot LLM behavior, which prior work (PrimeVul, SV-TrustEval-C, Risse et al., Weissberg et al., cited as related work) already covered -- for decoder-only LLM vulnerability detectors. The authors construct two datasets from a unified merge of DiverseVul, PrimeVul, and CVEFixes (436,489 samples total: 24,758 vulnerable, 19,418 fixed, 392,313 normal): V2P (vulnerable paired with its own patched version) and V2N (vulnerable paired with an unrelated normal function, sharing the same vulnerable side, 24,747 vulnerable functions in both). They fine-tune five 7B-8B open-source LLMs (Qwen3-8B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, DeepSeek-LLM-7B-Chat, DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B-Instruct) via LoRA (rank 8, alpha 32, 3 epochs, ms-swift framework) under two paradigms -- vanilla SFT and SFT with explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning distilled from an advanced LLM (GPT-5.4) given oracle-level context (CWE ID/description, commit message, repo). They define and operationally test a failure mode called the 'Semantic Trap' via three symptoms: (i) pairing-sensitive performance (V2N-trained high scores collapse on V2P), (ii) gap-dictated decisions (performance statistically tied to CodeBLEU textual/syntactic distance between vulnerable and patched code), and (iii) fragility to seven semantic-preserving code perturbations (renaming, dead-code injection, comments, whitespace, redundant functions, line breaks). A fourth component is an LLM-assisted taxonomy (8 categories, C1-C8) of reasoning failures built from 3,000 sampled failure explanations and then applied to classify all false positives/negatives.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the traditional security sense; the paper studies a reliability/evaluation-validity problem in ML-based vulnerability detectors. The 'threat' being probed is that fine-tuned LLM detectors may achieve high benchmark scores by exploiting superficial textual/functional-pattern shortcuts (surface-level lexical or structural cues, or the magnitude of textual difference between a vulnerable/benign pair) rather than genuinely reasoning about the underlying security-critical logic, producing detectors whose real-world reliability is illusory (e.g., massive false-positive rates when a patched version is functionally close to the vulnerable original).
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) all evaluated models are restricted to the 7B-8B parameter range due to compute constraints, so results may not generalize to larger models (though they argue, citing Weissberg et al., that the bottleneck is the learning paradigm rather than model scale); (2) commit-based vulnerability datasets (DiverseVul, PrimeVul, CVEFixes) are known to contain label noise, mitigated only partially via dedup and a label-priority heuristic. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own text: the RQ3 taxonomy is produced entirely by an LLM evaluator reading natural-language reasoning traces and comparing them to LLM-distilled 'ground truth' reasoning, not a direct probe of model internals -- the authors themselves flag this as characterizing 'the distribution of failure descriptions produced by this evaluator pipeline, not... the fine-tuned model's internal cognition.' The apparent disappearance of CodeBLEU-correlation under CoT fine-tuning is explicitly flagged by the authors as possibly a floor effect from lower recall rather than evidence of escaping the trap, and a Fisher z-test showed the paradigm-level contrast in correlation was statistically significant for only a subset of models.

datasets_used: Unified merged dataset (436,489 samples: 24,758 vulnerable, 19,418 fixed, 392,313 normal)
V2P dataset (Vulnerability-to-Patch, constructed by the authors)
V2N dataset (Vulnerability-to-Normal, constructed by the authors)
quantitative_results: Qwen3-8B pre- vs post-SFT (V2N test): F1 37.92% -> 67.43% (+29.5pp)
Qwen3-8B pre- vs post-SFT (V2P test): F1 38.43% -> 54.33% (+15.9pp)
Vanilla SFT V2N-to-V2N F1: Qwen3-8B 83.65%, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 83.88%
Vanilla SFT V2P-to-V2P F1: Qwen3-8B 66.06%, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct 66.39%
Vanilla SFT V2N-to-V2P: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct recall 96.98%, FPR 94.38%
CoT SFT V2N-to-V2P: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct FPR reduced to 64.60%
F1 degradation under 7x semantic-preserving augmentation: vanilla SFT 3.86-7.21pp drop vs CoT SFT <=1.12pp drop (deepseek-llm-7b-chat CoT even improved -0.91% on V2P)
V2P-to-V2P recall: deepseek-llm-7b-chat vanilla SFT 73.45% -> CoT SFT 46.31% (F1 65.49% -> 52.67%)
Vanilla SFT CodeBLEU-stratified F1: Qwen3-8B rises from 50.41% (>0.95 CodeBLEU) to 63.07% (0.75-0.80 CodeBLEU); deepseek-coder-6.7b-instruct rises from 41.91% to 58.77%
Vanilla SFT Spearman correlation (CodeBLEU vs F1): |sc| range 0.033-0.074, all p<0.05 (e.g., p=3.4084e-6 Qwen3-8B, p=3.3016e-5 deepseek-coder-6.7b-instruct)
CoT SFT Spearman correlation: sc near zero, all p>0.05 (e.g., p=0.349 Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, p=0.871 deepseek-llm-7b-chat)

8-category reasoning-failure taxonomy (C1-C8) applied across FP/FN for all 5 models and 4 cross-dataset settings; C1 (control-flow misinterpretation) and C5 (CWE/vulnerability-definition misapplication) are consistently among the most frequent categories
baselines_compared: Qwen3-8B pre-SFT vs post-SFT (used as the paper's own control, not an external baseline)
Vanilla SFT (without explicit reasoning) vs SFT with explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning -- the paper's own two training paradigms compared against each other, not external prior systems
